english_description: A NumericKernel is a small numeric model with a fixed number of components. It carries a finite set of weights (a normalized distribution over components) and a coupling matrix between component pairs whose off-diagonal entries are non-negative and whose rows sum to zero. From these, the kernel computes a normalized output matrix for a given scale, giving the relative contribution of each component pair after scaling, conditioned on the kernel's weights.

queries.english: How many components does the kernel have?
What is the weight of component i?
What is the coupling from component i to component j?
queries.rust_sig: fn coupling(&self, from: usize, to: usize) -> f64

queries.english: Fill the given buffer with normalized outputs for the given scale.
queries.rust_sig: fn normalize_into(&self, scale: f64, out: &mut [f64]) -> Result<(), KernelError>

constraints.english: The component count is finite and at least two.
Every weight is a finite value in [0, 1].
constraints.logic: forall i in 0..self.component_count(): self.weight(i).is_finite() && self.weight(i) >= 0.0 && self.weight(i) <= 1.0

constraints.english: The weights sum to one within WEIGHT_SUM_TOL.
Off-diagonal couplings are finite and non-negative.
constraints.logic: forall i,j in 0..self.component_count() where i != j: self.coupling(i,j).is_finite() && self.coupling(i,j) >= 0.0

constraints.english: Each diagonal coupling equals the negation of the row sum of off-diagonal couplings within ROW_SUM_TOL.
constraints.logic: forall i in 0..self.component_count(): abs(self.coupling(i,i) + sum(j in 0..self.component_count() where j != i: self.coupling(i,j))) <= ROW_SUM_TOL

constraints.english: Symmetric kernels satisfy weight-balanced symmetry within BALANCE_TOL.
constraints.logic: self.is_symmetric() implies forall i,j in 0..self.component_count(): abs(self.weight(i) * self.coupling(i,j) - self.weight(j) * self.coupling(j,i)) <= BALANCE_TOL

constraints.english: normalize_into returns an error when scale is non-finite or negative.
normalize_into returns an error when the output buffer length is not component_count squared.
When normalize_into succeeds, every entry of the output buffer is finite and within [0, 1].
constraints.logic: result.is_ok() implies forall k in 0..out.len(): out[k].is_finite() && out[k] >= 0.0 && out[k] <= 1.0

constraints.english: When normalize_into succeeds, each row of the output sums to one within ROW_STOCHASTIC_TOL.
constraints.logic: result.is_ok() implies forall i in 0..self.component_count(): abs(sum(j in 0..self.component_count(): out[i*self.component_count()+j]) - 1.0) <= ROW_STOCHASTIC_TOL

constraints.english: When normalize_into succeeds with scale equal to zero, the output is the identity matrix within IDENTITY_TOL.
constraints.logic: result.is_ok() && scale == 0.0 implies forall i,j in 0..self.component_count(): abs(out[i*self.component_count()+j] - (if i == j then 1.0 else 0.0)) <= IDENTITY_TOL

constraints.english: When normalize_into succeeds and the kernel is symmetric, the output satisfies weight-balanced symmetry within OUTPUT_BALANCE_TOL.
constraints.logic: result.is_ok() && self.is_symmetric() implies forall i,j in 0..self.component_count(): abs(self.weight(i) * out[i*self.component_count()+j] - self.weight(j) * out[j*self.component_count()+i]) <= OUTPUT_BALANCE_TOL

adversary_table.scenario: A concrete kernel declares component_count=1 (degenerate single-component case).
adversary_table.violates: the component count is at least two
adversary_table.resolution: The constructor of any concrete impl rejects with KernelError::InvalidComponentCount { count }.

adversary_table.scenario: Weights contain a NaN or negative value.
adversary_table.resolution: Construction-time validation rejects with KernelError::InvalidWeights. Query-side weight() must never observe a non-finite value because construction would have failed.

adversary_table.scenario: Caller passes an output buffer of the wrong size.
adversary_table.resolution: normalize_into returns KernelError::OutputBufferWrongSize { expected, actual } before touching the buffer.
